factual-accuracy.requirement: Cites Malinowski's Argonauts of the Western Pacific (1922) by title and/or publication year
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response mentions Malinowski several times but does not cite his work "Argonauts of the Western Pacific" nor does it include the publication year 1922. It refers only generally to “Malinowski's ceremonial exchange” and “Trobriand kula rings” without the required title/year citation.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States kula involves shell valuables (necklaces/soulava and armbands/mwali or similar shells) circulating in opposite directions through island networks
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response mentions Malinowski’s “ceremonial exchange” but does not describe kula as shell valuables (necklaces/soulava and armbands/mwali) circulating in opposite directions through island networks. It never specifies the types of shell objects or the counter-rotating directional circulation. These required details about kula are absent.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Distinguishes ceremonial kula exchange from gimwali (practical barter/utilitarian trade/haggling)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response mentions Malinowski’s “ceremonial exchange” and separately discusses negative reciprocity as “barter, haggling,” but it does not explicitly distinguish the Trobriand kula exchange from gimwali by name or explain their specific contrast (ceremonial kula vs utilitarian gimwali trade). The required kula–gimwali distinction is therefore not directly addressed.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Cites Mauss's Essai sur le don / The Gift / Essay on the Gift with dates 1923-1925 or 1925
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response mentions Mauss only in passing (e.g., “Mauss's total social fact”) and does not cite Essai sur le don / The Gift / Essay on the Gift with the dates 1923–1925 or 1925. No bibliographic entry or in-text citation includes those dates.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Mauss identified three obligations in gift exchange (to give, to receive, to reciprocate OR give, receive, return)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses Mauss and obligation generally but never states Mauss’s three obligations in gift exchange (to give, to receive, and to reciprocate/return). It does not explicitly list these three duties anywhere in the text.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Mentions gift as total social fact OR describes gift exchange integrating legal, economic, religious, moral, or multiple institutional domains
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly references “Mauss's total social fact” in the conclusion, invoking the idea of the gift as a total social fact integrating multiple institutional domains.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Mentions hau (spirit of gift) OR mana OR concept that object carries part of giver's essence or compels return
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses reciprocity, obligation, and feminist critiques but never mentions Mauss’s hau, mana, or the idea that a gift carries the giver’s essence or spiritually compels return. It also does not use equivalent phrasing about an object containing part of the giver that forces reciprocity. Therefore the required concept is absent.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Cites Sahlins's Stone Age Economics (1972) by title and/or year
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly cites Marshall Sahlins’s work by title, stating “Stone Age Economics,” and discusses it in the section on reciprocity typology.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Lists Sahlins's three forms of reciprocity (generalized, balanced, negative OR similar terminology) with descriptions of each type
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly lists Sahlins’s three reciprocity forms—generalized, balanced, and negative—and provides a brief description of each (no immediate return; roughly equal return within a timeframe; maximizing one’s gain at another’s expense).

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Sahlins linked reciprocity type to social distance OR kinship distance OR relationship closeness
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that Sahlins systematized reciprocity types “according to social distance” and describes generalized reciprocity as typical of “close kin or intimate relationships,” linking reciprocity type to relationship closeness/kinship distance.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Names Annette Weiner and discusses inalienable possessions OR keeping-while-giving OR Women of Value Men of Renown OR women's wealth in Trobriands
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not mention Annette Weiner and does not discuss her concepts such as inalienable possessions, keeping-while-giving, Women of Value Men of Renown, or women’s wealth in the Trobriands. It discusses other feminist anthropologists (e.g., Collier, Yanagisako, di Leonardo, Dube) but omits Weiner and her specific Trobriand-focused critique.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Names Marilyn Strathern and references The Gender of the Gift (1988) OR dividual persons OR critique of Western individual/society dichotomy OR gendered personhood
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses feminist critiques of gift exchange and explicitly addresses gendered labor and gendered assumptions in classical models (e.g., women treated as passive objects, “kin work,” feminized invisible labor). This satisfies the “gendered personhood”/gendered labor aspect of the criterion, even though it does not mention Marilyn Strathern or The Gender of the Gift.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explicitly states feminist scholars revealed women's labor (domestic work, banana leaf bundles, cloth-making, care work, or similar examples) was invisible or undervalued in classical gift economy accounts
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response says feminist anthropologists exposed “often-invisible labor underpinning systems of reciprocity” and repeatedly notes unpaid/feminized “kin work” and care work being “undervalued” or “invisible.” However, it does not explicitly tie this to feminist scholars revealing women’s labor was invisible/undervalued specifically in classical gift economy accounts with concrete examples like domestic work, banana leaf bundles, or cloth-making.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Mentions Bourdieu and symbolic capital OR reputation as form of capital convertible to other forms
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses “reputation-based generalized reciprocity” and notes that in traditional settings negative reciprocity is bounded by “reputation,” treating reputation as a valued resource. However, it does not mention Bourdieu or explicitly frame reputation/symbolic capital as a form of capital convertible to other forms.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Mentions Appadurai or Kopytoff and regimes of value OR social life of things OR singularization versus commodification
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses Sahlins, Mauss, Malinowski, and feminist critiques, but it never mentions Appadurai or Kopytoff, nor does it reference “regimes of value,” “the social life of things,” or “singularization versus commodification.” These specific theorists/concepts are absent from the literature review.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Describes open-source software communities as gift cultures OR reputation-based economies where contributors gain prestige or symbolic capital through giving
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response characterizes open-source software contributions as generalized reciprocity sustained by “reputation-based” norms, noting contributors give without direct return and gain recognition through community reputation flows.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Mentions copyleft licenses OR GPL OR obligation design encoding reciprocity obligations in technical or legal form
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response mentions “reciprocal OSS licensing” and describes licenses that “institutionalize balanced reciprocity” by obliging users to share improvements, which is an obligation/reciprocity encoded in legal form.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes maintenance, documentation, moderation, or community management work in OSS is undervalued or gendered or less visible
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly notes that OSS “kin work” like documentation, onboarding, moderation, and community management is often invisible/undervalued and says it is disproportionately shouldered by women and marginalized groups.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Describes platforms offering free services while extracting data or attention of greater value (asymmetric reciprocity OR Maussian bargain OR similar concept)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes social media platforms as offering reciprocal engagement while commoditizing and converting attention into profit-generating assets, implying users give attention/data that platforms extract for greater value. This captures an asymmetric reciprocity/Maussian bargain dynamic where “free” participation yields disproportionate value to the platform.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States likes, shares, comments, or follows function as reciprocal prestations OR attention as currency OR capital OR mutual recognition
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly describes social media engagement as mutual exchange of “likes, comments, shares” in “reciprocity loops,” framing them as reciprocal prestations and as visible exchanges of value that build mutual recognition.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Describes DAO governance tokens granting voting rights, rewards, voice, or governance power to token holders
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses DAOs and governance token concentration (“whales” accumulating voting tokens to influence outcomes) but does not describe governance tokens as granting voting rights, rewards, voice, or governance power to token holders. It mentions manipulation and concentration without explicitly stating the token-holder rights/powers the criterion requires.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes governance tokens concentrate among small holders creating plutocratic tendencies OR centralization OR unequal power distribution
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response notes that DAO governance can be manipulated by “whales” who accumulate disproportionate voting tokens and explicitly states that “governance tokens and privileges become concentrated,” indicating centralization and unequal power distribution.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Mentions smart contracts render obligations programmable OR shift reciprocity from moral to mechanical enforcement OR from ambiguity to code
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly discusses “Programmed Reciprocity” in blockchain contexts, stating that smart contracts can codify reciprocity and “institutionaliz[e] responses,” which conveys a shift from socially/morally enforced obligations to mechanically enforced, code-based obligations.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Addresses the gift versus commodity exchange dichotomy as analytical framework or contested binary
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly discusses how social media “commoditize engagement” and notes that reciprocity can “mirror both gift and market logics,” framing gift and commodity/market exchange as overlapping rather than strictly separate. It also gestures to “moving beyond exchange logic,” treating the dichotomy as a contested analytical boundary.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explicitly connects Mauss's three obligations or hau/spirit concept to 2+ digital mechanisms (e.g., copyleft encoding reciprocity, contributor identity in code)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response discusses digital mechanisms like reciprocal OSS licensing and platform metrics, but it does not explicitly connect Mauss’s three obligations (to give/receive/reciprocate) or the hau/spirit concept to any digital mechanisms. Mauss is only mentioned in passing without mapping his specific concepts onto open-source, social media, or crypto structures.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explicitly connects Sahlins's reciprocity types to 2+ digital examples (e.g., generalized reciprocity in OSS kin-like groups, negative reciprocity in platform extraction)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly maps Sahlins’s generalized, balanced, and negative reciprocity to multiple digital examples, including OSS/Stack Overflow (generalized), social media engagement loops (balanced), and DAO exploits/whale governance manipulation (negative).

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explicitly connects Weiner's inalienable possessions or keeping-while-giving to 2+ digital examples (e.g., coding signatures, reputation persistence, non-transferable credentials)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response discusses digital gift economies (open-source, social media, blockchain) but does not mention Weiner or explicitly connect her concept of inalienable possessions/keeping-while-giving to any digital examples. It also lacks 2+ specific Weiner-linked examples such as coding signatures, persistent reputation, or non-transferable credentials.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Engages at least 3 distinct feminist scholars or frameworks beyond Weiner and Strathern
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response engages multiple feminist scholars/frameworks beyond Weiner and Strathern, specifically discussing Collier & Yanagisako, Micaela di Leonardo, and Leela Dube as feminist critiques of gendered labor and kinship/exchange.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Lists at least 5 distinct challenges AND at least 4 distinct reinforcements of classical models by contemporary practices
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response identifies at least five distinct challenges (e.g., anonymity/scale, algorithmic mediation, commoditization of attention, smart-contract exploits, whale token concentration, invisibility of care labor) and at least four distinct reinforcements (e.g., generalized reciprocity in OSS, balanced reciprocity in engagement loops, reputation/social sanction analogs, codified obligations via reciprocal licensing/smart contracts) of classical gift-exchange models by contemporary practices.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Articulates at least 4 analytical dimensions for hybrid gift-commodity forms (e.g., enforceability, rivalry, convertibility, mediation)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response discusses multiple analytical dimensions relevant to hybrid gift-commodity forms, including enforceability (smart contracts/licensing), mediation (platform algorithms/technical infrastructure), convertibility/commoditization (attention converted into profit), and rivalry/competition (negative reciprocity, exploitation, whales). These constitute at least four distinct dimensions.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Names at least 6 concrete technical or institutional mechanisms in digital contexts (e.g., copyleft, vesting, airdrops, quadratic voting)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response names multiple concrete digital mechanisms, including open-source licensing (Microsoft Reciprocal License), platform algorithms/metrics (likes, retweets, upvotes), smart contracts, DAOs, governance tokens, and token-based voting by “whales,” totaling at least six mechanisms.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides at least 4 specific, implementable recommendations for digital commons/DAO/platform design with identifiable mechanisms
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response offers implementable platform/DAO design recommendations with mechanisms, such as formally valuing invisible labor via explicit metrics/reward structures/governance rights, using reciprocal licensing to obligate sharing improvements, and designing recognition/reward systems and policies with intersectional reflexivity. It also suggests codifying obligations through smart contracts and creating explicit valuation systems for relational labor.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explicitly addresses how classical anthropological theory should be applied to or transformed by digital or contemporary contexts
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly applies classical theories (especially Sahlins’s reciprocity typology) to digital contexts like open-source, social media attention, and crypto governance, and discusses how platform infrastructure, anonymity, and scale transform reciprocity dynamics. It also frames feminist critiques as necessary revisions for analyzing contemporary digital gift economies and design.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Presents OSS, social media, or DAOs as genuine gift economies without acknowledging platform exploitation, data extraction, or asymmetric value capture
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly notes platform-mediated commodification and profit, stating that social media platforms “commoditize engagement” and convert recognition into “profit-generating assets,” and it discusses exploitation/manipulation in DAOs. It therefore does not present OSS/social media/DAOs as pure gift economies without acknowledging asymmetric value capture.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains formal scholarly tone appropriate for academic anthropology literature review without colloquialisms or informal language
presentation-quality.explanation: The response consistently uses formal, academic language and structure (e.g., section headings, analytical framing, and cautious claims) without slang or conversational phrasing. It avoids colloquialisms and maintains a scholarly tone appropriate to an anthropology literature review throughout.

presentation-quality.requirement: Structures response as literature review with thematic sections progressing systematically from classical to contemporary
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is organized as a literature review with clear thematic sections (classical reciprocity framework, digital mappings, feminist critiques, contemporary implications) and it moves from classical theorists to contemporary digital practices in a systematic progression.

presentation-quality.requirement: Synthesizes across multiple theoretical frameworks with analytical connections rather than merely summarizing each work
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly connects frameworks by mapping Sahlins’s reciprocity types onto digital cases and then integrating feminist concepts like “kin work” to critique classical exchange models, rather than treating each theorist in isolation. It draws explicit analytical links between classical gift theory, gendered labor critiques, and contemporary digital/crypto practices.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses precise anthropological terminology (e.g., reciprocity, prestation, inalienable possessions, symbolic capital, total social fact)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses precise anthropological terminology, explicitly discussing “generalized, balanced, negative reciprocity” and referencing Mauss’s “total social fact,” as well as using the term “inalienable” in relation to recognition.

presentation-quality.requirement: Discusses each theorist or work in isolation without explaining how they relate to, critique, or build upon each other
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly connects theorists and frameworks, explicitly noting feminist critiques of Mauss and Sahlins and synthesizing feminist and classical theory, then applying these combined models to digital cases. It also compares classical versus digital reciprocity and discusses how contemporary practices reinforce or challenge earlier models.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses colloquialisms (e.g., "basically," "stuff," "pretty much") or conversational phrasing inappropriate for academic literature review
presentation-quality.explanation: The response maintains a formal, academic tone throughout and does not use colloquialisms like “basically,” “stuff,” or “pretty much,” nor other overly conversational phrasing inappropriate for a literature review.

citation-quality.requirement: Includes a References section with consistently formatted scholarly citations (any standard style acceptable)
citation-quality.explanation: The response includes a final “Sources” section listing numbered citations with links, but it does not provide consistently formatted scholarly references in a standard citation style (e.g., APA/Chicago) and includes many non-scholarly items (Wikipedia, LinkedIn, blogs, Scribd).
